hook: The cathedral's real medieval statues, brought indoors before the weather ate them
facts: Houses around 320 original sculptures and architectural fragments removed from the cathedral, mostly circa 1890-1910, to spare them pollution and falling stone.
Holds the 13th-century Ecclesia and Synagoga and the Tempter with the Wise and Foolish Virgins, which served as models for Freiburg and Basel.
Occupies the half-Gothic, half-Renaissance house of the Fondation de l'Œuvre Notre-Dame, first documented in 1281 and still maintaining the cathedral.
Created in 1931 by merging four Upper Rhenish collections; reopened 1956 after 1944 bomb damage.
w: What you crane up at on the cathedral facade are mostly copies. The originals stand here at eye level, lifted indoors before pollution and frost finished them off. The 13th-century Synagogue, blindfolded and broken-staffed, the leering Tempter offering his apple. Five centuries of weathered faces, suddenly close enough to read.

hook: A Dominican church and its priceless library burned in one 1870 night, rebuilt from scratch
facts: Stands on the site of a Dominican convent of 1260; the Lutheran cathedral parish moved in after Louis XIV expelled them in 1681.
Incendiary shells on the night of 24-25 August 1870 destroyed the old church and its library: about 400,000 volumes and 3,446 manuscripts lost.
Rebuilt 1874-1877 in Vosges pink sandstone, neo-Romanesque, by architect Emile Salomon, with a Merklin organ.
Holds the ledger stone of the Dominican mystic Johannes Tauler.
w: On one night in August 1870 Prussian shells set the old Dominican church ablaze, and with it a library of 400,000 books and 3,446 manuscripts went up irreplaceably. They rebuilt in pink Vosges sandstone, neo-Romanesque, finished by 1877. A whole congregation's memory, traded for fresh stone.

hook: 1.2 million stuffed and pickled specimens, including great auks and a coelacanth
facts: Rooted in the 1804 purchase of naturalist Johann Hermann's collections, first shown in his own apartment.
Housed since 1890-1893 in a purpose-built block of the University of Strasbourg on boulevard de la Victoire.
Conserves over 1.2 million specimens: roughly 900,000 insects, 200,000 molluscs, 18,000 birds, 10,000 mammals.
Reopened September 2025 after a six-year, 13-million-euro renovation; holds extinct great auks and passenger pigeons.
w: It began in 1804 as one dead doctor's curio cabinet, shown in his own flat. Two centuries on it runs to 1.2 million specimens, drawers of pinned beetles and shelves of pickled things, including a great auk and a passenger pigeon that no living eye will see again. Extinction, kept in glass.

hook: A church bricked down the middle for 330 years: Catholics one side, Protestants the other
facts: Christian worship attested on the site as early as the 4th century; the present church built 1381-1428.
Turned Protestant in 1529; in 1682 a wall split it, the choir to Catholics, the nave to Protestants.
A Catholic church was added perpendicular to the old choir after 1867 by architect Jean-Geoffroy Conrath as the population grew.
The dividing wall got a connecting door only on 12 October 2012; Protestant worship there has since ceased.
w: For 330 years a wall ran straight through this church: Catholics worshipped in the choir, Protestants in the nave, two faiths under one roof and never the twain. They cut a connecting door only in 2012. A whole Reformation feud, settled in masonry and finally undone with a doorway.

hook: A double-decker tomb of two brothers, and the church where Strasbourg sings Bach
facts: Begun early 14th century by the crusader knight Henri de Müllenheim, beside a mendicant convent on the Ill.
Keeps a unique two-storey double tomb of the brothers Philippe and Ulrich de Werd, carved around 1330-1344.
The oldest stained glass, in the entrance gallery, dates to 1345 and came from a Dominican convent.
Home since 1885 of the Chœur de Saint-Guillaume, founded by organist Ernst Münch, long famous for its Bach Passions.
w: Two brothers, two effigies, stacked one above the other in a strange two-storey tomb carved around 1340: Philippe and Ulrich de Werd, sleeping on separate floors. The crusader-founded church around them has the best acoustics in town, which is why Strasbourg has sung Bach's Passions here since 1885.

hook: The little square where suckling pigs were once sold, renamed every time the border moved
facts: Recorded in 1240 as the Holzmerket, the wood market, before becoming the suckling-pig market.
Changed names with every change of flag: Marché-aux-Porcs (1817), Neuer Fischmarkt (1872), Ferkelmarkt (1944), back to its present name in 1945.
The 16th-century Grande Boucherie, the old butchers' hall, stands to its west.
Ringed by half-timbered houses around an 18th-century wrought-iron wellhead.
w: Suckling pigs once changed hands on these cobbles, hence the splendid mouthful of a name. Watch it flip with the border: pig market under France, Neuer Fischmarkt under Germany, Ferkelmarkt in 1944, then snapped straight back in 1945. A square that wore whichever language won the war.

hook: A cellar deep as a church nave, where cathedral canons once stored their wine
facts: Vaulted cellars under the place de la Cathédrale, dimensions exceptional for the city, often likened to a church nave.
Stood on the medieval seat of the provostry of the cathedral's Grand Chapter, where canons cellared their wine.
Acquired and rebuilt in the 14th century by Conrad Gurtler, who gave it the name Gurtlerhoft.
Now an Alsatian winstub reached down a discreet stair off the cathedral square.
w: Step off the cathedral square, down an easily-missed stair, into a stone cellar so tall and wide it reads like an underground nave. Cathedral canons cellared their wine down here; in the 14th century one Conrad Gurtler took it over and left his name. You still come down for choucroute and a glass.

hook: A 13th-century Jewish ritual bath, found by accident under a modern building in 1985
facts: Uncovered in 1984-85 during renovation at the corner of rue des Juifs and rue des Charpentiers (20 rue des Charpentiers).
Probably built between 1200 and 1260, one of the oldest traces of Jewish architecture in Alsace.
A small sandstone chamber where a sunken pool drew directly on the groundwater table; called Zum Judenbad in the 16th century.
The only surviving Jewish structure from medieval Strasbourg; listed as a monument historique since 15 November 1985.
w: Renovators in 1984 broke through into a forgotten cellar and found a sandstone staircase dropping to a pool fed straight from the groundwater: a ritual bath dug between 1200 and 1260. The medieval Jewish community above it was destroyed; this sunken room, the only structure left of them, waited 700 years to be seen.

hook: A 1905 apartment block painted floor-to-roof with two giant Egyptians
facts: At 10 rue du Général Rapp in the Neustadt; permit granted 8 June 1905, finished 31 July 1906.
Commissioned by tailor Robert Goeres, designed by self-taught young architect François Scheyder.
A polychrome fresco by painter Adolphe Zilly (1906) runs the full height of the facade, two Egyptian figures among lotus and papyrus.
Mixes pharaonic motifs with Jugendstil; balconies carry stylised bats. Such exotic decor is rare in Strasbourg.
w: A Strasbourg tailor in 1905 wanted a building nobody would forget, and hired a self-taught architect with a taste for theatre. The result: two towering Egyptian figures frescoed the full height of the facade, lotus and papyrus, bats on the balconies. German Art Nouveau gone full pharaoh, on a quiet Neustadt street.

hook: The city's story inside its 1588 butchers' hall, around a 23-table model of itself
facts: Housed in the Grande Boucherie, the great butchers' hall built 1587-1588 to replace a 13th-century slaughterhouse.
Founded as a museum in 1920; fully renovated and reopened in 2013.
Centrepiece is the 1727 relief plan ordered by Louis XV: 23 fir-wood tables modelling the fortified city.
The plan was taken to Berlin in 1815 and only returned to Strasbourg in 1902.
w: The city tells its own story inside the hall where butchers worked from 1588. Its showpiece: a 1727 scale model of fortified Strasbourg, 23 tables of carved fir ordered by Louis XV, hauled off to Berlin in 1815 and not returned until 1902. A whole walled town, small enough to lean over and read.
